coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot additive engineering for efficient and stable carbon-based perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Tingting
Wan, Zhi
Tang, Hebing
Zhao, Chenhui
Lv, Shaoshen
Chen, Yonghua
Chen, Lixin
Qiao, Qiquan
Huang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Additive engineering is an effective method to manipulate formation of methyl ammonium metal halide perovskite film for high performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared from two different carbon sources named as A-CQD and CA-CQD respectively are applied as additives into CH3NH3PbI3 precursor solution via one-step processing. Film morphology and charge transport dynamics of perovskite films with different CQDs additives are investigated. It is found that the A-CQDs incorporated perovskite film has large grain size, excellent crystallinity, enhanced carrier transport ability and high hydrophobic surface. Carbon-based perovskite solar cell using the A-CQD additive shows increased cell performance with the maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 13.28%. While, PCE of the PSCs modified by the CA-CQD additive and without additive are 7.85% and 10.50% respectively. The A-CQD additive modified PSC also presents good long-term stability and maintains more than 90% of its initial PCE after 200 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cell
Additive
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Functional group
Stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of perovskite precursor solution and CQDs solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis schemes of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of A-CQD (a) and CA-CQD (b) obtained from two different carbon sources respectively; TEM images and the size distribution of A-CQD (c) and CA-CQD (d).
The FT-IR spectra of A-CQDs (a) and CA-CQDs (b); the survey XPS spectra and the C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of A-CQDs (c, e) and CA-CQDs (d, f).

One-step processing to prepare perovskite/CQDs films (a); proposed Mechanism of interaction of A-CQDs with perovskite (b); photographs of water contact angle tests of pristine perovskite film (c), perovskite/A-CQD film (d), perovskite/CA-CQD film (e).

FTIR spectra of A-CQDs, pristine perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 film, perovskite/A-CQDs annealed film, perovskite precursor solution, and perovskite/A-CQDs precipitated powder (a); FTIR spectra of CA-CQDs, pristine perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 film, perovskite/CA-CQDs annealed film, perovskite precursor solution, and perovskite/CA-CQDs precipitated powder (b).
Top-view SEM image of the pristine perovskite film (a), the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film (b), the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs film (c); AFM images of the three types of films (d–f).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the pristine MAPbI3 perovskite film, the perovskite with different concentration of A-CQD additive (0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt% and 0.2 wt%), the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs films (a); XRD patterns of the prinstine perovskite films, the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQD films, and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQD films (b); steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra (c) and time-resolved PL spectra (d) of the pristine perovskite film and the perovskite films with two types of CQDs.

J-V characteristics of carbon-based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) without additives, with 0.1 wt% A-CQD, and with 0.05 wt% CA-CQD additives (a); PCE distribution of PSCs with and without additives (b); Performance parameters of carbon-based PSCs with and without additives: J
sc (c), Voc (d), FF (e), PCE (f); Nyquist plot of three types of PSCs under dark condition (inset: the equivalent circuit diagram) (g); Long-term stability of three types of unpackaged PSCs tested under humidity of 40%–45% at room temperature (h).
Parameters fitted from TRPL spectra of the pristine perovskite film, the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film, and perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs film.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ∗The average carrier lifetime is obtained by the following formula.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of photovoltaic parameters of PSCs fabricated without additives, with 0.1% A-CQD, and with 0.05% CA-CQD additives.
EIS results of three different types of PSCs.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot additive engineering for efficient and stable carbon-based perovskite solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Frontiers Science Center for Flexible Electronics (FSCFE)
Shaanxi Institute of Biomedical Materials and Engineering (SIBME)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Additive engineering is an effective method to manipulate formation of methyl ammonium metal halide perovskite film for high performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared from two different carbon sources named as A-CQD and CA-CQD respectively are applied as additives into CH3NH3PbI3 precursor solution via one-step processing. Film morphology and charge transport dynamics of perovskite films with different CQDs additives are investigated. It is found that the A-CQDs incorporated perovskite film has large grain size, excellent crystallinity, enhanced carrier transport ability and high hydrophobic surface. Carbon-based perovskite solar cell using the A-CQD additive shows increased cell performance with the maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 13.28%. While, PCE of the PSCs modified by the CA-CQD additive and without additive are 7.85% and 10.50% respectively. The A-CQD additive modified PSC also presents good long-term stability and maintains more than 90% of its initial PCE after 200 h.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy conversion and storage are promising research areas to provide solutions for potential energy crisis in human society [1–5]. With great advantage of using abundant and clean solar energy, photovoltaic technology has gained steadily development for the last several decades. Organic-inorganic lead halide perovskite material has emerged as rising star for light harvesting, attributing to its superior light absorption property, high carrier mobility, and long charge diffusion length [6]. To date, Perovskite solar cell (PSC) has achieved 25.2% power conversion efficiency (PCE) since it was initially reported in 2009 [7–11]. Extensive researches have been conducted on manipulating morphology of perovskite films for high performance PSCs [12–15]. The main direction to improve quality of perovskite film is to enhance film surface coverage on the substrates, gain large grain size, and decrease amounts of grain boundaries etc [16]. Large crystal size of perovskite is benefit for higher charge carrier transformation efficiency due to fewer amounts of grain boundaries, where the non-radiative loss and trap-assisted non-radiative recombination are most likely taken place. Therefore, it is essentially important to enlarge grain size of perovskite via purposely adjusting crystallization of perovskite films. Researchers have developed various of strategies such as thermal annealing, post-processing technology and additive engineering etc to produce good perovskite films with large grain size [17–24].

Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, carbon nanodots and graphdiyne are efficient additives to modify the formation of perovskite. Owing to excellent optical and electrical properties of carbon nanomaterials, they have been applied into electron transporting layers (ETLs), active layer or interfacial layer etc in PSCs [21,25–32]. Hadadian et al. introduced the nitrogen-doped graphene (N-RGO) into the perovskite layer to increase solar cell performance [33]. The N-RGO slowed down the crystallization process of the perovskite and increased its grain size, thus the perovskite surface was passivated and the charge recombination was minimized. Zhang et al. used γ-graphdiyne quantum dots (GD QDs) as surface modifiers or dopants to modify functional layers of TiO2, CH3NH3PbI3 and Spiro-OMeTAD respectively [34]. The synergy advantage from three modified components attributed to higher PCE of 19.89% for the as-fabricated PSC than that without using GD QDs. Specifically, the perovskite active film blended with the GD QDs additive formed more efficient carrier transport channel to improve the performance of PSC compared with that without GD QDs additive. Hsu et al. adopted carbonized bamboo-derived carbon nanodots (CND) and urea as additives to modify the perovskite film, increasing PCE up to 16.47% [35]. The carboxylic acid and hydroxyl-rich CNDs were effectively bonded to the perovskite to passivate the defects at the grain boundaries. Ma et al. introduced carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the perovskite precursor solution to passivate uncoordinated lead ions at the grain boundaries [25]. They confirmed that the addition of CQDs can reduce non-radiative recombination by grain boundary passivation. Although researchers reported that additives of carbon nanomaterials can effectively improve the performance of PSCs, reports rarely studied the effect of functional groups of carbon additives on the perovskite formation. Different functional groups of additives play crucial roles in the formation of perovskite films. The functional groups can passivate the charged defects of perovskite during the crystallization process [36,37]. While, how the functional groups of CQDs additives affect the formation of perovskite films has not been revealed to the best of our knowledge.

Herein, two different CQDs named as A-CQD and CA-CQD were prepared from two carbon sources of acetone and anhydrous citric acid respectively. The different CQDs with and without nitrogen functionalities were synthesized and used as additives for the first time in PSCs. The photophysical properties of two CQDs were characterized by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The chemical bonding and functional groups of CQDs were identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The morphologies of perovskite films modified by the two respective CQDs additives were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atom force microscopy (AFM). The as-fabricated hole transport layer (HTM)-free carbon-based PSCs using the A-CQD additive presented better power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 13.28% than that of the CA-CQDs of 7.85%. While the PSCs fabricated by the pristine perovskite film showed 10.50% PCE as reference. It is found that the A-CQDs additive interacted with the perovskite CH3NH3PbI3, retarded the growth rate of crystal grains, thus the grain size of perovskite film was enlarged. The A-CQDs modified perovskite film also had facilitated carrier transport and enhanced photovoltaic performance. The long-term stability of the A-CQDs incorporated PSCs was greatly improved. Our work demonstrated that A-CQD with favorable functional groups is a promising additive to improve performance of PSC.

Fig. 1 a&b show the synthesis schemes of two carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared from two different carbon sources of acetone (A) and anhydrous citric acid (CA) respectively. The CQDs were named as A-CQD and CA-CQD according to their respective precursors. The A-CQD was obtained by simply treating acetone with NaOH for several days. It was explained that Aldols reaction was taken place under strong base condition, and then oligomer chain was polymerized, and further crimped and entangled to form CQDs as illustrated in Fig. 1a [38]. The CA-CQD was synthesized by reacting anhydrous citric acid with ethylenediamine, followed by carbonization to form the CQDs [39,40]. The reaction process is shown in Fig. 1b. The as-synthesized A-CQD can be well dispersed in ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), but it has poor solubility in water. While, CA-CQD has good solubility in water and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), but insoluble in ethanol. The different solubility of A-CQDs and AC-CQDs indicates the distinct nature of their surface ligands. TEM images of A-CQD and CA-CQD were shown in Fig. 1c&d respectively. Two CQDs all present uniformly distributed dot shape, and the average diameters of A-CQDs and CA-CQDs are 4 nm and 2 nm respectively as shown in the inserted figure. It confirms that both A-CQD and CA-CQD were successfully prepared. Optical properties of two CQDs were characterized by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in Fig. S1. The absorption peak of A-CQDs is located at 293 nm, and two typical absorption bands of CA-CQDs at 240 nm and 345 nm can be observed respectively. In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs are highly dependent on the excitation wavelength as shown in the inserted Fig. S1. As the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 300 nm to 480 nm, the emission peaks of the A-CQDs solution changed from 486 nm to 555 nm. For CA-CQDs, the emission peaks moved from 446 nm to 510 nm, when the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 300 nm to 440 nm. Two CQDs demonstrate distinct optical properties because of the different surface properties.

In order to study surface chemistry of two CQDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to examine their difference. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, hydroxyl, carbonyl, alkyl groups can be found from both of the two CQDs samples. The dissimilarity of the two CQDs is that the A-CQD has stronger stretching vibrations of the C–H group than the CA-CQD, which located at 2958 cm−1 and 2871 cm−1. It indicates that the A-CQD may have more alkyl groups than the CA-CQD. Another big difference is that CA-CQD has strong NH2- bending vibration at 1390 cm−1 and the –NH– vibration at 1114 cm−1, while none of the amine group can be observed in the A-CQDs. XPS analysis of the two CQDs in Fig. 2c-f identified the existence of amine group in the CA-CQDs sample. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of Fig. 2e, the peaks at 284.8, 286.4, 288.2 eV of A-CQD ascribe to three different types of carbon atoms including sp2 carbon atoms, alcoholic (C–OH) and carbonyl (CO) carbon atoms [38]. For CA-CQD sample, XPS analysis in Fig. 2f reveals that the carbon atoms are graphitic or aliphatic (CC and C–C), oxygenated, and nitrous [39]. The FTIR and XPS results provide clear clue to understand the surface nature of the two CQDs. The CA-CQDs might have more hydrophobic alkyl groups than A-CQDs, while the A-CQDs contain amine groups. Reports state that the amine group would coordinate with the dangling bond of Pb2+ on the surface of perovskite film [37]. This distinct nature of CQDs would affect perovskite film formation when incorporating the CQDs additives in the perovskite precursor for one-step processing.

Fig. 3a presents the process to form CH3NH3PbI3/CQDs film via one-step spin coating. The proposed mechanism of CQDs interacted with perovskites was illustrated in Fig. 3b. The different functional groups of CQDs interact differently with the CH3NH3PbI3. A-CQDs rich in alkyl groups might form water resistance barrier. CA-CQDs attached with NH2 groups would interact with the Pb2+ atom in the precursor solution. To test the surface properties of the as-prepared perovskite/CQD film, water contact angle measurement was carried out to examine the perovskite films without additive, with A-CQDs and with CA-CQDs at optimized concentration, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3c–e, the water contact angle of the pristine CH3NH3PbI3 film, the 1 wt% A-CQDs/CH3NH3PbI3 film and the 0.05 wt% CA-CQDs/CH3NH3PbI3 film are 30.99° ± 2.61°, 52.78° ± 1.05° and 36.79° ± 0.74°, respectively. The perovskite/A-CQDs film shows much improved hydrophobic property compared with these of the pristine perovskite film and the perovskite/CA-CQDs film. The perovskite/A-CQDs film may benefit for preventing from moisture invade. It confirms that the incorporation of the A-CQDs into the CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite film leads to enhanced moisture resistance. Thus the stability of perovskite film can be enhanced by adding A-CQD additive.

To further understand the interaction mechanism between perovskites and two different CQDs, FTIR spectroscopy was carried out to detect changes of the functional groups after blending perovskites with CQDs as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The partially enlarged spectrum with the wavelength of 1000–2000 nm is shown on the right. Two types of samples were tested, i.e. the perovskite precursor solution blended with CQDs before annealing (named as the powder of perovskite/CQD) and the annealed perovskite/CQD film. The powder of perovskite/CQD was obtained by adding anti-solvent diethyl ether to precipitate the solid. It was observed that the CO stretching vibration peak of the powder of perovskite/A-CQDs was moved from 1707 cm−1 to 1683 cm−1 compared with the pristine A-CQDs as shown in Fig. 4a. The movement of the CO stretching vibration peak to the low frequency range indicates the migration of the electron clouds from CO to O–Pb, proving the intermolecular interaction between the CO and the Pb2+ [20,35]. The CO stretching vibration peak in the perovskite/A-CQDs annealed film was moved from 1707 cm−1 to 1693 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 4a (right). It moved back to a higher frequency range compared with the perovskite/A-CQDs powder with the value of 1683 cm−1. The movement of the CO groups revealed that the interaction between the perovskite and A-CQDs. Such interaction between A-CQDs and the PbI2 would delay the crystal growth of perovskite films, larger perovskite crystals might form and the crystallinity of the perovskite film might be improved [20,35,41]. It is likely to occur at the grain boundaries of perovskite films [25]. Moreover, the powder of perovskite/CA-CQDs and the annealed perovskite/CA-CQDs films were also studied by FTIR spectroscopy in Fig. 4b. The peak value of CO vibration of the perovskite/CA-CQDs powder was moved from 1656 cm−1 to 1641 cm−1. With an electron-withdrawing effect originated from the –NH2 group, the electron negativity of the CO groups was reduced, resulting in a weakened coordination between CO with Pb2+ in the perovskite/CA-CQDs films. The shift of the CO vibration showed that interaction of perovskite with CA-CQDs also existed. For the perovskite/CA-CQDs annealed film, the shift of the CO vibration peak kept constant with that of the powder of perovskite/CA-CQDs, indicating that thermal annealing did not affect as obviously as that of the perovskite/A-CQDs film.

Film morphologies of perovskites with addition of A-CQDs and CA-CQDs were characterized to further identify the effect of the CQDs additives. Fig. 5a–c shows the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the pristine perovskite film, the perovskite film with 0.1 wt% A-CQD additive and the perovskite film with 0.05 wt% CA-CQD additive, respectively. The average grain size of above three films are 170 nm, 245 nm and 160 nm, respectively. The perovskite film with 0.1 wt% A-CQDs showed the largest grain size among all three samples. The grain size of the perovskite film with 0.05 wt% of CA-CQDs were slightly reduced compared with the pristine perovskite film. The grain size distribution of three perovskite films was shown in Fig. S2. It can be observed that the grain size of the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film is in the range of 180–300 nm. Increased grain size of perovskites can reduce the total number of grain boundaries in the perovskite film, promoting carrier transport and reducing carrier recombination [36]. The change in the grain sizes of the perovskite can be attributed to the interaction between PbI2 and CQDs. The incorporation of the CQDs additive can inhibit the crystallization rate of the perovskite, thus affect the grain sizes. It was found that the two CQDs presented significantly different phenomenon on the perovskite crystal growth. When A-CQDs were used as additives, PbI2 can strongly interact with the CO in the A-CQDs to delay the rapid crystallization of perovskites, increasing the grain sizes. However, the interaction of the CA-CQD additive with the Pb2+ might be relatively weak, resulting in smaller average crystal grain. Surface roughness of the different perovskite films were characterized by atomic force microscope (AFM) as shown in Fig. 5d–f. The average roughness Ra of the pristine perovskite films, the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs films, and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs films were 10.3 nm, 14.5 nm and 8.55 nm respectively. The perovskite film with 0.1 wt% A-CQDs had a rougher surface than the other two samples. The coarser perovskite film would lead to more effective light scattering and absorption [35,42,43].

Fig. 6a shows the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of the pristine perovskite film and the perovskite films blended with two types of CQDs respectively. The absorbance of the perovskite/A-CQD film was increased after adding the A-CQD compared with the pristine perovskite film, reaching to its maximum absorbance value at the concentration of 0.1 wt%. The enlarged view in Fig. 6a showed the trend of the perovskite/A-CQDs films was initially increased, and then decreased when the concentration of A-CQD was continuously increased to 0.2 wt%. While, the absorption intensity of the perovskite/CA-CQD film declined significantly after just adding a low concentration of 0.05 wt% CA-CQDs. The addition of CQDs may lead to the change in the viscosity of the perovskite precursor, causing different thickness of the perovskite films [24]. Compared to the CA-CQD additive, the light absorbance of the perovskite/A-CQD film was obviously improved, thus it may attribute to high Jsc for the as-fabricated PSCs with A-CQD.

Fig. 6b shows the XRD patterns of the pristine perovskite film, the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film, and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs film. XRD peaks located on 14.1°, 28.5°, 31.9°, 40.7° and 43.2° were respectively corresponded to the crystal planes of (110), (220), (114), (224) and (314) of the MAPbI3 orthogonal lattice [35]. No diffraction peak of PbI2 can be detected at 12.5° for all three films, confirming the completed conversion of the PbI2 to the MAPbI3 for all three conditions. The peak intensity at (110) and (220) for the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film was increased. In order to quantify crystallinity of the as-prepared perovskite films, the full-width half-maxima (FWHM) of the (110) plane was measured and evaluated by using the Scherrer equation. The pristine perovskite film and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQD film showed almost identical value of the FWHM. The FWHM of perovskite film with the 0.1 wt% A-CQDs reduced significantly, indicating that the perovskite film with 0.1 wt% A-CQD additive promoted better crystal crystallinity and higher average grain size [44]. The XRD results are in good agreement with the film morphology images in Fig. 5. The XRD results confirmed that the A-CQDs can be used as effective additive to obtain large grain sized perovskite films.

The steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured to study carrier transport properties of the perovskite films blended with different CQDs. The perovskite/CQDs films were prepared by spin-coating different types of perovskite precursor solution directly on the glass substrates via one-step deposition. As shown in Fig. 6c, the PL intensity of the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQDs film was increased significantly compared with those of the pristine perovskite film and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs film. Since the insulting glass substrate was used, the PL intensity is mainly related to the recombination of the excitons in the film. The PL intensity of the perovskite film with 0.1 wt% A-CQDs additives was notably increased compared with the pristine perovskite films. It revealed that the A-CQD incorporated perovskite film had a reduced non-radiative recombination due to the fewer defects in the modified film [45]. Moreover, increase of the PL intensity in the perovskite/A-CQD film confirmed better film crystallinity. It is in good consistent with the XRD results in Fig. 6b. In contrast, the perovskite/CA-CQD film showed a dramatically reduced PL emission, showing its severe recombination in the defect-rich perovskite/CA-CQD film. Similar phenomenon can be also found in our previous reports [22,24].

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of the three films were shown in Fig. 6d. Biexponential rate law model was used to fit the PL decay. Typically, the fast decay time τ1 is associated with the non-radiative recombination caused by the charge trapping defect state, and the slow decay time τ2 is related to the bimolecular radiation recombination [36]. Table 1 lists the fitting parameters of the three samples. The τ1 and τ2 of the pristine perovskite film, the perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQD film and the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQD film were 6.95 ns (38.39%) and 78.50 ns (61.61%), 6.52 ns (25.59%) and 93.66 ns (74.41%), 5.74 ns (21.03%) and 18.53 ns (78.97%), respectively. The corresponding amplitudes are in parentheses. With the addition of the A-CQDs, decrease in the fraction of τ1 and an increase of the value of τ2 can be observed. It proves that the A-CQDs can reduce the non-radiative recombination of the perovskite films. In addition, τ1 and τ2 for the perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQDs film were both reduced compared with the pristine perovskite film. It indicates that the defect density of the perovskite/CA-CQDs films is increased, thus the carrier recombination is easily to occur. The average lifetime τavg was calculated, and the τavg of the pristine perovskite films, perovskite/0.1 wt% A-CQD, and perovskite/0.05 wt% CA-CQD were 74.76 ns, 91.62 ns and 17.56 ns, respectively. It shows clearly that the A-CQD additive has positive effect for the carrier transport in the as-blended perovskite/A-CQD film.
τ avg
=
∑
A i
τ i 2
∑
A i
τ i

Carbon-based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with hole transport layer (HTM)-free structure were fabricated to compare the CQDs effect (see Table 2 ). The device architecture is fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO)/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/MAPbI3/Carbon. Fig. 7 a shows the J-V characteristic curves of PSCs irradiated by the AM 1.5 G solar simulator under a light intensity of 100 mW/cm2 at reverse scan. Table 2 summarizes photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs with and without CQD additives. Each batch was obtained from 20 to 30 individual cells to eliminate the error. Concentration of the A-CQD additive in the PSCs was optimized as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1. Solar cells made by 0.1 wt% A-CQDs incorporated perovskite film achieved 10.74 ± 0.56% PCE. The highest PCE of 13.28% was obtained by adding 0.1 wt% A-CQD, which is nearly 21% higher than that of the PSC using pristine perovskite. PCE of PSCs prepared without any CQDs additives gained 8.86 ± 0.64%. While, PSCs fabricated by the 0.05 wt% and 0.1 wt% CA-CQD additives were compared, and PCE of these two PSCs were decreased to less than 8% and 4% for conditions of 0.05 wt% and 0.1 wt% respectively. It proved that very small amount of CA-CQD additive caused negative effect on the photovoltaic performance of PSCs. Fig. 7b shows histogram of the PCE distribution of three different types of PSCs. The PSC using the 0.1 wt% A-CQDs has significant improvement in PCE than these of the CA-CQD and the pristine perovskite film. In addition, the individual photovoltaic parameters of three types of PSCs without additive, with A-CQDs, and with CA-CQDs were drawn for comparison respectively. Fig. 7c–f shows the photovoltaic plots of Jsc, Voc, FF and PCE. The repeatability of solar cells based on the pristine and with A-CQDs additives is relatively high. It shows that A-CQD additive based PSCs gained enhanced photovoltaics parameters compared with the other two types of PSCs. While, the PSCs made by the CA-CQDs additives had quite low Jsc value and big variation of FF value.

In order to quantitatively evaluate dynamics, resistance and capacitance of PSCs modified by different CQD additives, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted under dark condition as shown in Fig. 7g. The EIS results are summarized in Table 3. The inserted figure in Fig. 7g represents the equivalent circuit consisting of a series resistance (Rs), a recombination resistance (Rrec), and a parallel capacitor (Crec) [25]. The Rs value of 0.1 wt% A-CQDs based PSCs is 7.53 Ω, which is reduced by nearly half of that without additive (14.98 Ω). A smaller series resistance indicates better carrier transport of the PSCs. Moreover, the 0.1 wt% A-CQDs incorporated PSCs showed a higher Rrec value of 664.56 Ω than the other two types of solar cells, i.e. 418.77 Ω for the pristine PSC, and 376.20 Ω for the CA-CQDs based PSCs. Increase in Rrec indicates that the charge recombination process in perovskite films is effectively suppressed, benefitting for Voc of PSCs [46]. The Rrec of the 0.05 wt% CA-CQDs modified PSCs becomes smaller in comparison with the pristine PSCs. It suggests the charge recombination of the CA-CQDs incorporated PSCs becomes even more severe than the pristine PSCs.

Fig. 7h shows the long-term stability of the as-fabricated PSCs tested under a relative humidity of 40–45% at room temperature. PCE of the PSCs assembled by the pristine and 0.05 wt% CA-CQDs modified perovskite films remained 36.6% and 80.2% of the initial PCE value after storing for 200 h respectively. Notably, PSC using 0.1 wt% A-CQDs additive maintained more than 90% of its initial PCE for the first 200 h. In addition, 57% of its initial value was kept after storing for 1400 h, taking from the average PCE from four individual cells. There also have other reports about enhanced long-term stability of CQDs blended PSCs [25,35]. The results herein demonstrated that the A-CQDs modified PSCs showed superior stability compared with the CA-CQDs modified PSCs. Improvement in long-term stability of PSC with A-CQDs might be due to the interaction of A-CQD additive with Pb2+ in the perovskite film, thus increasing the grain size and reducing the defect density of the perovskite film. On the other hand, A-CQD additive is rich in hydrophobic alkyl groups. The hydrophobic property of the perovskite/A-CQDs films is improved compared with the pristine perovskite and the perovskite/CA-CQD films. Therefore, the perovskite/A-CQD film can effectively against moisture invading, attributing to a better stability.

Two carbon quantum dots (CQDs) named as A-CQDs and CA-CQDs were synthesized from two different carbon sources of acetone and citric acid, and they were used as additives to manipulate perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 formation respectively. The two CQDs showed different effect on the perovskite film formation. The A-CQDs incorporated perovskite film has the largest grain size, the highest crystallinity and the best carrier transport property, least non-radiative recombination losses among the all three perovskite films. Alkyl groups in A-CQDs improved the hydrophobic property of the perovskite film. While, the CA-CQDs showing an obviously negative effect on carrier recombination in the perovskite/CA-CQDs films. The carbon-based perovskite solar cells (PSC) with 0.1 wt % A-CQD additive achieved 13.28% PCE with the comparison of the pristine PSC of 10.50% and the 0.05 wt% CA-CQD blended PSC of 7.85%. The long-term stability of PSCs blended with A-CQDs has been greatly improved. This work reveals the difference of CQD additives on the formation of perovskite films. It provides meaningful understanding about how the CQDs additive would affect the perovskite film formation and the performance of the responding perovskite solar cells.

Two CQDs of A-CQD and CA-CQD were synthesized from different carbon sources. A-CQD was obtained from Aldol reaction of acetone according to the literature method [38]. In detail, 8 g NaOH and 40 mL acetone were mixed under vigorously magnetic stirring for 1 h. The mixture was kept under ambient conditions for 120 h. After the reaction, HCl solution (1 M) was added to neutralize the reactant. The product was precipitated by centrifuge, and then washed by deionized water for several times. The as-obtained A-CQD solid was freeze-dried for 48 h. The CA-CQD was synthesized via a modified method in our previous report [40]. Briefly, 4.204 g anhydrous citric acid (≥99.5%), 1.34 mL ethylenediamine (≥99.0%) and 40 mL of deionized water were mixed by stirring. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 5 h. A brownish black solution was obtained after naturally cooling to room temperature. The purification process was done by using dialysis bag to remove the impurities. The final CA-CQD powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of perovskite precursor solution and CQDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: The perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 159 mg MAI (>99.5%, Xi’an p-OLED Corp.) and 461 mg PbI2 (>99.99%, Xi’an p-OLED Corp.) in mixed solvents of 78 mg N, N-dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (99.7%, Macklin) and 600 mg N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (99.8%, Aladdin). The perovskite precursor solution was stirred in N2 atmosphere overnight. Stock solution of CQDs (20 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving CQDs in DMF solution. To form CQD doped perovskite solution, the diluted CQD solution was added into the original perovskite precursor solution and stirred for 20 min to form homogeneous mixture. The weight percentage of the CQD in the doped perovskite solution was considered by taking the relative weight ratio of CQD to the mass of MAI.

Conductive FTO glass substrates were ultrasonically cleaned in bath of detergent, deionized water, acetone, isopropanol and anhydrous ethanol for 15 min sequentially. Multiple layers of nanocrystalline TiO2 films were coated on the cleaned FTO glass to form the electron transport layer. In detail, TiO2 compact layer (c-TiO2) was prepared by spin-coating 0.15 M titanium diisopropoxide dis (acetylacetonate) (Sigma-Aldrich, 75 wt% in isopropanol) in 1-butanol (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.8%) on the FTO substrate at 2000 rpm for 30 s and annealed at 125 °C for 10 min. Suspension of diluted mesoporous TiO2 (dyesol, 18NR-T) by ethanol (1: 3.5, wt:wt) was spin-coated on top of the c-TiO2 layer at 5000 rpm for 30 s and gradually annealed up to 500 °C and kept for 30 min. Perovskite precursor solution was spin-coated on top of the TiO2 films at 4000 rpm for 20 s after filtration. During the spin-coating process, antisolvent diethyl ether was dripped quickly onto the perovskite film. Then the perovskite film was annealed at 95 °C for 10 min. A commercial carbon paste (Guangzhou Seaside Technology Development Co., Ltd.) was doctor-bladed onto the perovskite film, followed by heating at 100 °C for 15 min to finish the device fabrication.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption of CQD solution was obtained using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). The photoluminescence (PL) of CQD solution was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, F-4600). The optical properties of perovskite films were tested by an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, U–3900H) and a steady state/transient fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, PICOQUANT, FluoTime 300). FTIR was done by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, BRUKER, TENSOR 27) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. XPS was tested using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Shimadzu, Axis Supra). TEM was carried out by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200X). Ultra-high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI, Verios G4) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Dimension Fast Scan and Dimension Icon) were performed to test film morphology. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of perovskite film was carried out by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu, 7000) at 40 kV and 40 mA at an operating rate of 5°/min. The current density-voltage (J-V) curve of solar cells was measured by Keithley 2401 source meter including a solar simulator (SXDN-150 E, Nowdata) with 100 mW/cm2 light intensity. A standard Si solar cell was used for calibration. The effective area of the solar cell is 0.09 cm2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was done by impedance analyzer (Princeton 4000) from 100 Hz to 1 MHz frequency at a bias voltage of 0.8 V in dark conditions. The Nyquist curve was fitted by the ZView software.

Tingting Xu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhi Wan: Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hebing Tang: Investigation. Chenhui Zhao: Investigation. Shaoshen Lv: Data curation. Yonghua Chen: Data curation. Lixin Chen: Data curation. Qiquan Qiao: Formal analysis. Wei Huang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely thank financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972274), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (310201911cx035), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M653730), Natural Science Basis Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (2019JM-328), and the Seed Foundation of Innovation and Creation for Graduate Students in Northwestern Polytechnical University (ZZ2019035, ZZ2019231). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of NPU for the SEM and AFM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

J-V characteristics of the perovskite solar cells fabricated without and with different concentration of A-CQDs additives; Grain size distribution of the CQDs modified perovskite films. Supplementary data to this article can be found in the support information.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.157784.
